texts.text: (Dated: February 14, 2023)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The stability condition for Hawkes processes and their non-linear extensions usually relies on the condition that the mean intensity is a finite constant. It follows that the total endogeneity ratio needs to be strictly smaller than unity. In the present note we argue that it is possible to have a total endogeneity ratio greater than unity without rendering the process unstable. In particular, we show that, provided the endogeneity ratio of the linear Hawkes component is smaller than unity, Quadratic Hawkes processes are always stationary, although with infinite mean intensity when the total endogenity ratio exceeds one. This results from a subtle compensation between the inhibiting realisations (mean-reversion) and their exciting counterparts (trends).
texts.text: The stability condition for Hawkes processes and their non-linear extensions usually relies on the condition that the mean intensity is a finite constant. It follows that the total endogeneity ratio needs to be strictly smaller than unity. In the present note we argue that it is possible to have a total endogeneity ratio greater than unity without rendering the process unstable. In particular, we show that, provided the endogeneity ratio of the linear Hawkes component is smaller than unity, Quadratic Hawkes processes are always stationary, although with infinite mean intensity when the total endogenity ratio exceeds one. This results from a subtle compensation between the inhibiting realisations (mean-reversion) and their exciting counterparts (trends).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: Hawkes processes, Endogeneity ratio, Stationarity, QHawkes, ZHawkes
texts.text: Keywords: Hawkes processes, Endogeneity ratio, Stationarity, QHawkes, ZHawkes

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hawkes processes have been used in various fields to model endogenous dynamics, where past activity triggers more activity in the future. Indeed, Hawkes processes were found to be relevant to capture the selfexcited nature of the dynamics in biological neural networks [1, 2], in financial markets [3, 4], in seismologic activity (earthquakes) [5], and also in crime rates or riot propagation [6, 7]. Standard linear Hawkes processes are basically akin to a branching process, where each event generates on average n H 'child' events. The process cannot be stable when n H > 1 , as events proliferate exponentially with time, and no stationary state can ever be reached. When n H < 1 , on the other hand, the average event rate reaches a finite constant that diverges as (1 -n H ) -1 as n H → 1 . 1 Therefore, for standard Hawkes processes, the stability criterion coincides with the condition that the event rate remains finite.
texts.text: Hawkes processes have been used in various fields to model endogenous dynamics, where past activity triggers more activity in the future. Indeed, Hawkes processes were found to be relevant to capture the selfexcited nature of the dynamics in biological neural networks [1, 2], in financial markets [3, 4], in seismologic activity (earthquakes) [5], and also in crime rates or riot propagation [6, 7]. Standard linear Hawkes processes are basically akin to a branching process, where each event generates on average n H 'child' events. The process cannot be stable when n H > 1 , as events proliferate exponentially with time, and no stationary state can ever be reached. When n H < 1 , on the other hand, the average event rate reaches a finite constant that diverges as (1 -n H ) -1 as n H → 1 . 1 Therefore, for standard Hawkes processes, the stability criterion coincides with the condition that the event rate remains finite.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As argued by Kanazawa and Sornette in [10, 11], non-linear Hawkes processes allow one to combine both excitatory and inhibitory effects, and can describe an even broader range of phenomena. One special class of such non-linear processes, called Quadratic Hawkes Processes (QHP), were introduced and studied in [12]. On top of the standard Hawkes feedback, a signed process (price changes in the context of [12]) also contributes to the current activity rate, in a quadratic way (see below for a more precise definition). On top of the n H child events triggered by the Hawkes feedback, the new quadratic feedback contributes to n Q extra child events. What was shown in [12] is that the average event rate now diverges when the total endogeneity ratio n H + n Q reaches unity. From this result, it was concluded that the QHP is only stationary when n H + n Q < 1 .
texts.text: As argued by Kanazawa and Sornette in [10, 11], non-linear Hawkes processes allow one to combine both excitatory and inhibitory effects, and can describe an even broader range of phenomena. One special class of such non-linear processes, called Quadratic Hawkes Processes (QHP), were introduced and studied in [12]. On top of the standard Hawkes feedback, a signed process (price changes in the context of [12]) also contributes to the current activity rate, in a quadratic way (see below for a more precise definition). On top of the n H child events triggered by the Hawkes feedback, the new quadratic feedback contributes to n Q extra child events. What was shown in [12] is that the average event rate now diverges when the total endogeneity ratio n H + n Q reaches unity. From this result, it was concluded that the QHP is only stationary when n H + n Q < 1 .

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: ∗ michael.benzaquen@polytechnique.edu
texts.text: ∗ michael.benzaquen@polytechnique.edu

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 1 The case n H = 1 is special and can also reach a stationary state with finite mean intensity when the immigration rate is zero, see [8], or infinite mean intensity but finite typical (or median) intensity, as shown in [9].
texts.text: 1 The case n H = 1 is special and can also reach a stationary state with finite mean intensity when the immigration rate is zero, see [8], or infinite mean intensity but finite typical (or median) intensity, as shown in [9].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The aim of this note is to show that such a conclusion was too hasty. In fact, we claim that whenever n H < 1 the QHP is always stationary, albeit with a diverging mean intensity in the case n H + n Q > 1 . More precisely, the distribution density of the local intensity decays asymptotically as a power-law, with an exponent that becomes smaller than 2 whenever n H + n Q > 1 , such that the average intensity diverges. Stationary processes with infinite mean intensity also appeared very recently in the context of non-linear Hawkes processes in Refs. [10, 11]. Intuitively, the mixed excitatory/inhibitory effects encoded by QHP allows one to avoid the exponential run-away of Hawkes processes when n H > 1 , while describing a highly intermittent process with divergent mean intensity.
texts.text: The aim of this note is to show that such a conclusion was too hasty. In fact, we claim that whenever n H < 1 the QHP is always stationary, albeit with a diverging mean intensity in the case n H + n Q > 1 . More precisely, the distribution density of the local intensity decays asymptotically as a power-law, with an exponent that becomes smaller than 2 whenever n H + n Q > 1 , such that the average intensity diverges. Stationary processes with infinite mean intensity also appeared very recently in the context of non-linear Hawkes processes in Refs. [10, 11]. Intuitively, the mixed excitatory/inhibitory effects encoded by QHP allows one to avoid the exponential run-away of Hawkes processes when n H > 1 , while describing a highly intermittent process with divergent mean intensity.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: II. HAWKES PROCESSES: DEFINITION
texts.text: II. HAWKES PROCESSES: DEFINITION

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A. Linear Hawkes Process
texts.text: A. Linear Hawkes Process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hawkes processes were first introduced to model earthquake dynamics [5], in particular the propensity of seismic activity to cluster in time. This same phenomenon is observed in financial markets, where trading activity and volatility tend to cluster in time.
texts.text: Hawkes processes were first introduced to model earthquake dynamics [5], in particular the propensity of seismic activity to cluster in time. This same phenomenon is observed in financial markets, where trading activity and volatility tend to cluster in time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A Hawkes process ( N t ) t ≥ 0 is an inhomogenous Poisson process (meaning that its intensity is time dependent), the intensity of which is defined with the past realisations of the process according to the following equation:
texts.text: A Hawkes process ( N t ) t ≥ 0 is an inhomogenous Poisson process (meaning that its intensity is time dependent), the intensity of which is defined with the past realisations of the process according to the following equation:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: λ t = λ ∞ + ∫ t -∞ φ ( t -u )d N u , (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where λ t is the local intensity, i.e. probability that d N t is equal to 1 is λ t d t ; λ ∞ is called the baseline intensity and φ ( · ) the influence kernel, from which one obtains the endogeneity ratio n H as the norm of φ :
texts.text: where λ t is the local intensity, i.e. probability that d N t is equal to 1 is λ t d t ; λ ∞ is called the baseline intensity and φ ( · ) the influence kernel, from which one obtains the endogeneity ratio n H as the norm of φ :

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For an univariate linear Hawkes process to be stable, one needs n H < 1 . In fact, this is the necessary condition for the mean intensity ¯ λ := E ( λ t ) to be finite.
texts.text: For an univariate linear Hawkes process to be stable, one needs n H < 1 . In fact, this is the necessary condition for the mean intensity ¯ λ := E ( λ t ) to be finite.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the main limitation of Hawkes processes for financial applications is that the stationary distribution of local intensities, P ( λ ) , has 'thin tails' that cannot reproduce the fat tailed distribution of activity/volatility observed in most financial time series. Furthermore, as noted in [12], linear Hawkes processes cannot reproduce the violations of time reversal symmetry observed in financial time series [13].
texts.text: One of the main limitation of Hawkes processes for financial applications is that the stationary distribution of local intensities, P ( λ ) , has 'thin tails' that cannot reproduce the fat tailed distribution of activity/volatility observed in most financial time series. Furthermore, as noted in [12], linear Hawkes processes cannot reproduce the violations of time reversal symmetry observed in financial time series [13].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B. Quadratic Hawkes
texts.text: B. Quadratic Hawkes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To overcome the limitations of linear Hawkes processes, Blanc et al. [12] introduced a quadratic extension of Hawkes processes, where the intensity is dependent on both past activity ( d N t ) and past price returns d P t := ϵ t ψ d N t , where ϵ t = ± 1 is an unbiased random sign, independently chosen at each price change, and ψ is the size of elementary price changes. The QHP is now defined as:
texts.text: To overcome the limitations of linear Hawkes processes, Blanc et al. [12] introduced a quadratic extension of Hawkes processes, where the intensity is dependent on both past activity ( d N t ) and past price returns d P t := ϵ t ψ d N t , where ϵ t = ± 1 is an unbiased random sign, independently chosen at each price change, and ψ is the size of elementary price changes. The QHP is now defined as:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: λ t = λ ∞ + 1 ψ ∫ t -∞ L ( t -s )d P s + 1 ψ 2 ∫ t -∞ ∫ t -∞ Q ( t -s, t -u )d P s d P u , (3)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where L ( · ) is called the leverage kernel (breaking the d P t → -d P t symmetry) and Q ( · , · ) the quadratic kernel. Note that L and Q must be such that the quadratic form in d P s is positive definite, see [12].
texts.text: where L ( · ) is called the leverage kernel (breaking the d P t → -d P t symmetry) and Q ( · , · ) the quadratic kernel. Note that L and Q must be such that the quadratic form in d P s is positive definite, see [12].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since d P 2 t = ψ 2 d N t , a purely diagonal Q (to wit: Q ( t -s, t -u ) = φ ( t -s ) δ ( s -u ) ) recovers the standard Hawkes kernel. New effects arise when considering nondiagonal contributions to Q , see below.
texts.text: Since d P 2 t = ψ 2 d N t , a purely diagonal Q (to wit: Q ( t -s, t -u ) = φ ( t -s ) δ ( s -u ) ) recovers the standard Hawkes kernel. New effects arise when considering nondiagonal contributions to Q , see below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Taking the expectation of Eq. (3) provides an exact equation for the mean intensity ¯ λ , provided it exists. Noting that E [ ϵ s ] = 0 and E [ ϵ s ϵ u ] = δ ( s -u ) , one readily obtains:
texts.text: Taking the expectation of Eq. (3) provides an exact equation for the mean intensity ¯ λ , provided it exists. Noting that E [ ϵ s ] = 0 and E [ ϵ s ϵ u ] = δ ( s -u ) , one readily obtains:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hence, ¯ λ = λ ∞ / (1 -n ) is positive and finite whenever n < 1 , but becomes formally negative when n > 1 which was interpreted in [12] as a regime where the QHP becomes non-stationary, in analogy with what happens in the case of standard linear Hawkes processes. As we shall see below, this conclusion is not always warranted.
texts.text: Hence, ¯ λ = λ ∞ / (1 -n ) is positive and finite whenever n < 1 , but becomes formally negative when n > 1 which was interpreted in [12] as a regime where the QHP becomes non-stationary, in analogy with what happens in the case of standard linear Hawkes processes. As we shall see below, this conclusion is not always warranted.

(4)) as the sum of the two terms: the Hawkes endogeneity ratio n H := || φ || and the Zumbach endogeneity ratio n Z = || z 2 || . Naively, the stability of the ZHawkes process should read:
texts.text: represents the trend-induced (Zumbach) component. Correspondingly, one can then obtain the endogeneity ratio n (defined in Eq. (4)) as the sum of the two terms: the Hawkes endogeneity ratio n H := || φ || and the Zumbach endogeneity ratio n Z = || z 2 || . Naively, the stability of the ZHawkes process should read:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, our simulations show that one can have a non explosive process when n ≥ 1 provided n H < 1 . In this case, the QHP is stationary but with an infinite mean intensity ¯ λ . The stability of the QHP for n Z + n H > 1 arises from the fact that the inhibiting realisations of Z t (corresponding to locally mean-reverting behaviour of the price) are compensating the exciting ones (corresponding to local trends), see the detailed discussion in [10].
texts.text: However, our simulations show that one can have a non explosive process when n ≥ 1 provided n H < 1 . In this case, the QHP is stationary but with an infinite mean intensity ¯ λ . The stability of the QHP for n Z + n H > 1 arises from the fact that the inhibiting realisations of Z t (corresponding to locally mean-reverting behaviour of the price) are compensating the exciting ones (corresponding to local trends), see the detailed discussion in [10].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the following, we show the results of our simulations of an univariate ZHawkes process (ZHP) with n H = 0 and n Z > 1 , and an exponential Zumbach kernel z ( · ) . We also simulate the corresponding continuous limit of the ZHawkes process, as worked out in [12], and reinterpret the analytical results of Blanc et al. in the context of the present discussion.
texts.text: In the following, we show the results of our simulations of an univariate ZHawkes process (ZHP) with n H = 0 and n Z > 1 , and an exponential Zumbach kernel z ( · ) . We also simulate the corresponding continuous limit of the ZHawkes process, as worked out in [12], and reinterpret the analytical results of Blanc et al. in the context of the present discussion.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: III. UNIVARIATE ZHP: NUMERICAL RESULTS
texts.text: III. UNIVARIATE ZHP: NUMERICAL RESULTS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we provide evidence that the intensity of a simulated ZHawkes Process with n Z > 1 is stationary. In order to simulate a ZHP process, we adapt the thinning algorithm presented by Ogata (1981) in [15]. The Zumbach kernel z ( · ) is chosen to be a pure exponential, such that Z t can be interpreted as an exponential moving average of past returns, i.e. a proxy for the recent trend in prices. More precisely, we set
texts.text: In this section, we provide evidence that the intensity of a simulated ZHawkes Process with n Z > 1 is stationary. In order to simulate a ZHP process, we adapt the thinning algorithm presented by Ogata (1981) in [15]. The Zumbach kernel z ( · ) is chosen to be a pure exponential, such that Z t can be interpreted as an exponential moving average of past returns, i.e. a proxy for the recent trend in prices. More precisely, we set

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: z ( s ) = γ e -ωs , γ := √ 2 n Z ω

texts.label: text
texts.orig: with n Z = 2 , ω = 0 . 03 and a total simulation time of T = 10 6 . We also set n H = 0 , i.e. no Hawkes feedback, and choose the baseline rate to be λ ∞ = 0 . 5 .
texts.text: with n Z = 2 , ω = 0 . 03 and a total simulation time of T = 10 6 . We also set n H = 0 , i.e. no Hawkes feedback, and choose the baseline rate to be λ ∞ = 0 . 5 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 1 represents the whole time series of the simulated intensity, which shows that the process does not explode and looks stationary. More precisely, we find that the survival function of the process E (Λ) := P [ λ ≥ Λ] does not significantly evolve with time, see Fig. 2. In particular, the distribution does not become significantly 'fatter' as time increases, as would be expected if the process was on an explosive path. (Note that a formal Kolmogorov-Smirnov test of this statement is not straightforward because the λ t 's are correlated in time, see [16]). Finally, and most importantly, the empirical distribution function very nicely matches the theoretical prediction of [12], namely:
texts.text: Figure 1 represents the whole time series of the simulated intensity, which shows that the process does not explode and looks stationary. More precisely, we find that the survival function of the process E (Λ) := P [ λ ≥ Λ] does not significantly evolve with time, see Fig. 2. In particular, the distribution does not become significantly 'fatter' as time increases, as would be expected if the process was on an explosive path. (Note that a formal Kolmogorov-Smirnov test of this statement is not straightforward because the λ t 's are correlated in time, see [16]). Finally, and most importantly, the empirical distribution function very nicely matches the theoretical prediction of [12], namely:

The results fluctuate somewhat, but there is no systematic trend towards a fatter tail at longer times. (8). Parameters are: n Z = 2 , T = 10 6 , ω = 0 . 03 , n H = 0 and λ ∞ = 0 . 5 .

texts.orig: The conclusion is that the ZHP, at least in the continuum limit, always reaches a stationary distribution when n H < 1 , albeit the tail of the distribution of λ t is a power-law, with an exponent that becomes smaller than unity when n H + n Z > 1 , i.e. leads in that case to a divergent mean intensity.
texts.text: The conclusion is that the ZHP, at least in the continuum limit, always reaches a stationary distribution when n H < 1 , albeit the tail of the distribution of λ t is a power-law, with an exponent that becomes smaller than unity when n H + n Z > 1 , i.e. leads in that case to a divergent mean intensity.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: V. CONCLUSION
texts.text: V. CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we have revisited the properties of Quadratic Hawkes processes in the strong feedback regime. Based on numerical simulations and analytical results, we have argued that a new regime exists, where the process reaches a stationary state with an infinite mean intensity. Such a regime is absent for standard (linear) Hawkes processes: the stability of the process requires the mean intensity to be finite, except in the critical case [9]. As argued by Kanazawa & Sornette [10, 11], non-linear Hawkes processes allow for a rich phenomenology, with inhibitory and excitatory effects that can balance each other in a subtle way, resulting in a highly fluctuating, but non-explosive process, for which they provide several other examples.
texts.text: In this work, we have revisited the properties of Quadratic Hawkes processes in the strong feedback regime. Based on numerical simulations and analytical results, we have argued that a new regime exists, where the process reaches a stationary state with an infinite mean intensity. Such a regime is absent for standard (linear) Hawkes processes: the stability of the process requires the mean intensity to be finite, except in the critical case [9]. As argued by Kanazawa & Sornette [10, 11], non-linear Hawkes processes allow for a rich phenomenology, with inhibitory and excitatory effects that can balance each other in a subtle way, resulting in a highly fluctuating, but non-explosive process, for which they provide several other examples.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: QHP naturally lead to a power-law tail distribution for the local intensity, with an exponent that can become less than unity, in which case the mean intensity diverges. The resulting price process then converges to a Lévy stable process with an infinite variance, in spite of the fact that elementary price changes are strictly bounded (and, in our example, equal to ± ψ ). As such, this regime is not directly relevant to financial markets, since price returns, although fat-tailed, have a finite variance. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, our analysis is far from mathematically rigorous. Although the results of [12] for the continuous time version of the Z-Hawkes process are suggestive (see section IV), a more formal proof of the stationarity of Quadratic Hawkes processes with general kernels and in the infinite mean intensity regime would be welcome. An extension of the present discussion to multivariate Quadratic Hawkes processes is under way.
texts.text: Finally, our analysis is far from mathematically rigorous. Although the results of [12] for the continuous time version of the Z-Hawkes process are suggestive (see section IV), a more formal proof of the stationarity of Quadratic Hawkes processes with general kernels and in the infinite mean intensity regime would be welcome. An extension of the present discussion to multivariate Quadratic Hawkes processes is under way.
